site.title: In the Absence of a Soapbox
site.subtitle: Rants and Revelations of a Thirty-Something Single Mum
site.author: Kel Wright

book.title: In the Absence of a Soapbox
book.subtitle: Rants and Revelations of a Thirty-Something Single Mum
description: It starts at sixteen. Pregnant. No plan. Entirely out of her depth. It ends at thirty, with 20 kilograms gained, two terrible relationships survived, one daughter raised mostly well, and a genuinely surprising amount of self-knowledge accumulated along the way.

In between: dating apps and dumpster fires. Open relationships and labiaplasties. Woolworths bras that function as structural engineering. A solo trip to the Drakensberg that achieved nothing except confirmed that healing doesn't come in scenic packages.
This is not a book about having it figured out. It is a book about what happens when you don't, told with the kind of honesty that makes you recognise yourself in someone else's worst decisions.

authorBio: At 30, I suddenly find I have a lot to say about life. Not only about my own life, mind you, but other peoples' too. I have opinions on topics as varied as sex, parenting, home décor, and the unforgivable presence of fruit in a savoury salad.

I am not yet forty. I also hold a very prestigious, ultra-important, super-adventurous, definitely-not-boring job at an insurance company. This makes me entirely qualified to offer opinions and fully expect them to be taken seriously.

The book covers the years between 16 and 30. The ones in which I became a mother, lost myself in a couple of men who were not worth the trouble, gained 20 kilograms and stopped apologising for it, and slowly — very slowly — started to figure out what I actually thought about things.
Since writing it, I've run six half marathons, completed the Warrior Race, severed my PCL, and had PCL reconstruction surgery.
I write because some experiences don't improve with silence. They just get heavier. And because the flopped brownie is better than the real deal.
houseRules: No unsolicited advice. No unsolicited anatomy.
Profanity is allowed. So are opinions.
Take what you need, leave what you don't.
beforeYouRead: This isn't a guide, a manifesto, or a place to get your life sorted.
It's a record — written with candour, reflection, and enough humour to survive reliving my own questionable decisions.
Profanity appears without warning. So do opinions. Both are intentional.
Kindness is welcome. Curiosity too. Cruelty is not interesting.
Take what resonates. Leave the rest exactly where you found it.
